text: Sentence-based image annotation is an important aspect of image understanding. It is necessary for many vision applications such as sentence-based image search. Image search has its corresponding task of ranking candidate sentences for sentence-based image annotation. In this work, we present a new benchmark, a dataset called Flickr30k, which contains 8,000 images paired with 5 captions describing various objects and activities shown in the images. In addition, we propose and compare several systems that work very well in ranking captions to describe an image. All the systems that we compare use very basic features that can be obtained with minimal supervision. Therefore, the good performance that they achieve on the Flickr30k dataset verifies the importance of having multiple captions for training an image description system and of considering the syntax and semantics of the captions. We also perform an experimental evaluation of different metrics that can be used to measure the quality of ranking descriptions of an image or the relevance of captions w.r.t. a sentence. We give recommendations on how to collect sufficient human judgements in an economical fashion and in large quantities, and on how to expand our dataset. Our results indicate that ranking metrics are significantly better than the single response evaluation metrics. Therefore, our proposed evaluation metric is fully automatic.
